title: document.getElementById()
summary: Finds a single element on the page by its unique id attribute. This is one of the most common ways to grab a specific element so you can change its content, style, or listen for events. If no element with that id exists, it returns null.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: A string matching the id attribute of the element you want to find, e.g. 'header' or 'submit-btn'.

title: document.getElementsByClassName()
summary: Returns a live HTMLCollection of all elements on the page that have a specific class name. 'Live' means if you add or remove elements from the DOM, the collection updates automatically. Note: you need to loop through the result — it's not an array.

parameters.name: className
parameters.description: A string with the class name(s) to search for, e.g. 'card' or 'card featured'.

title: document.getElementsByTagName()
summary: Returns a live HTMLCollection of all elements with a given HTML tag name (like 'p', 'li', 'div'). Useful when you want to do something to all elements of a particular type on the page.

parameters.name: tagName
parameters.description: An HTML tag name as a string, e.g. 'p', 'img', 'li'. Use '*' to match all elements.

title: document.querySelector()
summary: Finds the first element on the page that matches a CSS selector. This is very powerful because you can use any CSS selector — including classes, IDs, pseudo-classes, attribute selectors, and more — to pinpoint exactly the element you need.

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: Any valid CSS selector string, e.g. '#nav', '.card:first-child', 'input[type="email"]'.

title: document.querySelectorAll()
summary: Returns a static NodeList of all elements matching a CSS selector. Unlike getElementsByClassName, this is not live — it's a snapshot. You can use forEach() on a NodeList, or spread it into an array for full array methods.

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: Any valid CSS selector string. Matches all elements that match, not just the first.

title: element.addEventListener()
summary: Attaches a function (called a callback or event handler) to an element so it runs when a specific event happens — like a click, keypress, or form submission. This is the modern, preferred way to handle events in JavaScript.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: The event type as a string, e.g. 'click', 'input', 'submit', 'keydown', 'mouseover'.

parameters.name: listener
parameters.description: The function to call when the event fires. Receives an event object as its first argument.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Optional. An options object, e.g. { once: true } to auto-remove the listener after the first fire.

title: element.removeEventListener()
summary: Removes an event listener that was previously added with addEventListener(). You must pass the exact same function reference — so the listener function needs to be stored in a named variable, not an anonymous arrow function.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: The event type string — must match the one used in addEventListener().

parameters.name: listener
parameters.description: The same function reference that was passed to addEventListener().

title: element.innerHTML
summary: Gets or sets the HTML content inside an element, including any HTML tags. Setting innerHTML replaces all the element's content. Be careful: never put user-supplied text directly into innerHTML as it can create security vulnerabilities (XSS). Use textContent for plain text instead.

parameters.name: value (setter)
parameters.description: An HTML string to set as the element's inner content. Tags are parsed as real HTML.

title: element.textContent
summary: Gets or sets the plain text content of an element. Unlike innerHTML, it doesn't parse HTML tags — any tags you set are shown as literal text, making it safe to use with user-supplied content. Great for updating labels, headings, and display values.

parameters.name: value (setter)
parameters.description: A plain text string. Any HTML tags are treated as text, not markup.

title: element.style
summary: Directly accesses an element's inline CSS styles. You can read or set individual style properties using camelCase versions of CSS property names (e.g. 'background-color' becomes 'backgroundColor'). These inline styles override stylesheet rules.

parameters.name: propertyName
parameters.description: A CSS property in camelCase, e.g. 'color', 'fontSize', 'backgroundColor', 'marginTop'.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value as a string, including units where required, e.g. '16px', 'red', 'flex'.

title: element.classList
summary: Provides methods to add, remove, toggle, and check CSS classes on an element. This is the cleanest way to change an element's appearance via JavaScript, because your styles stay in CSS while JS just switches classes on and off.

parameters.name: .add(className)
parameters.description: Adds one or more class names to the element.

parameters.name: .remove(className)
parameters.description: Removes one or more class names from the element.

parameters.name: .toggle(className)
parameters.description: Adds the class if it's missing, removes it if it's present.

parameters.name: .contains(className)
parameters.description: Returns true if the element has the class, false if not.

parameters.name: .replace(old, new)
parameters.description: Replaces an existing class with a new one.

title: element.setAttribute()
summary: Sets the value of an attribute on an element. If the attribute already exists, its value is updated. If it doesn't exist, it is created. Useful for setting href, src, disabled, aria-* attributes, and data-* attributes dynamically.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name as a string, e.g. 'href', 'src', 'disabled', 'aria-label'.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to set, always as a string.

title: element.getAttribute()
summary: Retrieves the value of a specific attribute from an element. Returns the value as a string, or null if the attribute doesn't exist. Commonly used to read data-* attributes, href values, and custom attributes.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name to retrieve, e.g. 'data-id', 'href', 'value'.

title: element.appendChild()
summary: Adds a node (usually a new element) as the last child of a parent element. If the node already exists somewhere in the document, it is moved to the new position. Use with createElement() to dynamically build page content.

parameters.name: node
parameters.description: The DOM node to append. Usually a new element created with document.createElement().

title: element.removeChild()
summary: Removes a specified child element from a parent element. The removed element is returned, so you can reuse it if needed. You must call this on the parent, passing the child you want to remove.

parameters.name: child
parameters.description: The child node to remove. Must be a direct child of the element you call removeChild() on.

title: document.createElement()
summary: Creates a new HTML element in memory (but doesn't add it to the page yet). After creating it, you set its content and attributes, then add it to the DOM using appendChild(), prepend(), or insertBefore(). This is how you dynamically build page content with JavaScript.

parameters.name: tagName
parameters.description: The HTML tag name for the element to create, e.g. 'div', 'p', 'button', 'li', 'img'.

title: console.log()
summary: Prints information to the browser's developer console. Essential for debugging — use it to check the values of variables, see if code is running, and understand what's happening in your program. Open the console with F12 or right-click > Inspect > Console.

parameters.name: ...data
parameters.description: One or more values to log. These can be strings, numbers, objects, arrays, or any JavaScript value.

title: alert()
summary: Shows a pop-up dialog box with a message and an OK button. It blocks the page until the user clicks OK. Useful for quick messages during development, but avoid using it in finished websites as it interrupts the user experience.

parameters.name: message
parameters.description: The text to display in the alert dialog. Non-string values are converted to strings.

title: prompt()
summary: Shows a dialog box with a text input field, asking the user to enter a value. Returns the text the user typed as a string, or null if they clicked Cancel. Great for learning, but avoid in real projects — custom input forms are much better for user experience.

parameters.name: message
parameters.description: The text label shown above the input field.

parameters.name: default
parameters.description: Optional. A default value pre-filled in the input field.

title: confirm()
summary: Shows a dialog with OK and Cancel buttons. Returns true if the user clicks OK, or false if they click Cancel. Useful for asking the user to confirm a destructive action, like deleting an item.

parameters.name: message
parameters.description: The question to display in the dialog box.

returns: boolean (true for OK, false for Cancel)

title: setTimeout()
summary: Runs a function once after a specified delay in milliseconds. It's non-blocking — the rest of your code continues running while the timer counts down. Returns a timer ID that you can use with clearTimeout() to cancel it before it fires.

parameters.name: callback
parameters.description: The function to run after the delay.

parameters.name: delay
parameters.description: The number of milliseconds to wait before running the function. 1000ms = 1 second.

title: setInterval()
summary: Repeatedly runs a function at a fixed interval (in milliseconds). Unlike setTimeout which fires once, setInterval keeps firing until you stop it with clearInterval(). Great for clocks, countdowns, and animation polling.

parameters.name: callback
parameters.description: The function to run repeatedly.

parameters.name: delay
parameters.description: The number of milliseconds between each call.

title: clearTimeout()
summary: Cancels a setTimeout() timer before it fires. You must pass the timer ID that was returned by setTimeout(). If the timer has already fired, calling clearTimeout() has no effect.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The timer ID returned by the setTimeout() call you want to cancel.

title: clearInterval()
summary: Stops a setInterval() timer from continuing to fire. Pass the ID that was returned by setInterval(). Always store the interval ID in a variable so you can stop it later — otherwise the interval runs forever (even after navigating away on some browsers).

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The interval ID returned by the setInterval() call you want to stop.

title: fetch()
summary: Makes an HTTP request to a URL and returns a Promise. Used to load data from APIs or servers without refreshing the page. You chain .then() calls (or use async/await) to handle the response. The response body isn't automatically parsed — you need to call .json() or .text() on it.

parameters.name: url
parameters.description: The URL to request as a string, e.g. 'https://api.example.com/data'.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Optional. An object with settings like method ('GET', 'POST'), headers, and body for the request.

title: JSON.parse()
summary: Converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object (or array). JSON is the standard format for sending data between a server and a browser. You'll use this whenever you receive data from an API as a text string and need to work with it as a JavaScript value.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: A valid JSON string to parse. Throws a SyntaxError if the string is not valid JSON.

returns: Object, Array, string, number, boolean, or null

title: JSON.stringify()
summary: Converts a JavaScript object or array into a JSON string. You need this when sending data to a server (e.g. in a fetch POST request body) or when saving a JavaScript object to localStorage.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The JavaScript value to convert — usually an object or array.

parameters.name: replacer
parameters.description: Optional. A function or array to filter properties. Pass null to include everything.

parameters.name: space
parameters.description: Optional. A number of spaces for pretty-printing, e.g. 2 for readable output.

title: parseInt()
summary: Converts a string to a whole (integer) number. It reads digits from the start of the string and stops when it hits a non-digit character. If the string doesn't start with a number, it returns NaN (Not a Number). Always pass 10 as the second argument to ensure base-10 parsing.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to parse, e.g. '42', '3.7', '100px'.

parameters.name: radix
parameters.description: The base of the number system. Use 10 for standard decimal numbers.

example:
console.log(parseInt('42', 10));    // 42
console.log(parseInt('3.9', 10));   // 3 (truncates, not rounds)
console.log(parseInt('100px', 10)); // 100 (ignores 'px')
console.log(parseInt('abc', 10));   // NaN

title: parseFloat()
summary: Converts a string to a floating-point (decimal) number. Similar to parseInt but preserves the decimal part. Useful when reading numeric values from form inputs or API data that arrive as strings.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to parse, e.g. '3.14', '9.99', '100.5px'.

example:
console.log(parseFloat('3.14'));     // 3.14
console.log(parseFloat('9.99 GBP')); // 9.99
console.log(parseFloat('abc'));      // NaN

title: Math.random()
summary: Returns a random decimal number between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). On its own it's not that useful, but multiplied and floored it can generate random integers in any range. A staple for games, quizzes, and randomised content.

title: Math.floor()
summary: Rounds a number down to the nearest whole number. 'Floor' means towards negative infinity — so 4.9 becomes 4, and -1.1 becomes -2. Almost always used together with Math.random() to generate random integers.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number to round down.

title: Math.ceil()
summary: Rounds a number up to the nearest whole number. 'Ceil' means towards positive infinity — so 4.1 becomes 5, and -1.9 becomes -1. Useful when you need to ensure you always round up, for example when calculating the number of pages needed for pagination.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number to round up.

example:
console.log(Math.ceil(4.1));  // 5
console.log(Math.ceil(4.9));  // 5
console.log(Math.ceil(-1.9)); // -1

// Pages needed for 25 items, 10 per page:
const pages = Math.ceil(25 / 10); // 3

title: Math.round()
summary: Rounds a number to the nearest whole number using standard rounding rules — .5 and above rounds up, below .5 rounds down. The most intuitive of the Math rounding methods for everyday use.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number to round.

example:
console.log(Math.round(4.4)); // 4
console.log(Math.round(4.5)); // 5
console.log(Math.round(4.9)); // 5

// Round a price to 2 decimal places:
const price = Math.round(9.9999 * 100) / 100; // 10

title: Array.push()
summary: Adds one or more items to the end of an array and returns the new length of the array. This modifies the original array — it doesn't create a copy. One of the most commonly used array methods for building up lists of data.

parameters.name: ...items
parameters.description: One or more values to add to the end of the array.

returns: number (the new length of the array)

title: Array.pop()
summary: Removes the last item from an array and returns it. This modifies the original array. Useful in stack data structures where you want to process items in last-in, first-out order.
returns: The removed element, or undefined if the array is empty

title: Array.map()
summary: Creates a new array by calling a function on every item in the original array and collecting the return values. The original array is not changed. Great for transforming data, e.g. converting an array of objects into an array of HTML strings.
syntax: const newArray = array.map(item => /* return transformed item */);

parameters.name: callback(element, index, array)
parameters.description: A function called for each element. Its return value becomes the corresponding element in the new array.

title: Array.filter()
summary: Creates a new array containing only the items for which a test function returns true. The original array is not changed. Perfect for searching and narrowing down datasets, e.g. filtering a list to only show items matching a search term.
syntax: const filtered = array.filter(item => /* return true to keep */);

parameters.name: callback(element, index, array)
parameters.description: A test function. If it returns true for an element, that element is included in the result.

returns: New Array (a subset of the original)

title: Array.forEach()
summary: Runs a function once for every item in an array. Unlike map(), it doesn't return a new array — it's used purely for side effects like logging, updating the DOM, or sending requests. It's a clean alternative to a traditional for loop.

parameters.name: callback(element, index, array)
parameters.description: A function called for each element. Any return value is ignored.

title: Array.find()
summary: Returns the first element in an array that satisfies a test function. If no element matches, it returns undefined. Useful when you have an array of objects and need to find one specific item, like finding a product by its ID.
syntax: const found = array.find(item => /* return true when found */);

parameters.name: callback(element, index, array)
parameters.description: A test function. find() returns the first element for which this returns true.

title: String.split()
summary: Splits a string into an array of substrings, using a specified separator. The original string is not changed. Useful for parsing comma-separated values, splitting sentences into words, or breaking up structured text data.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: A string or regular expression to split on. If omitted, the whole string becomes a single-item array.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Optional. Maximum number of splits to make.

title: Array.join()
summary: Joins all elements of an array into a single string, with a specified separator between each element. Note: join() is actually an Array method, not a String method. It's the opposite of split() — use them together to process text.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: A string to place between each element. Defaults to a comma if omitted.

example:
const words = ['Hello', 'World', 'JavaScript'];
console.log(words.join(' '));  // 'Hello World JavaScript'
console.log(words.join(', ')); // 'Hello, World, JavaScript'
console.log(words.join('-'));  // 'Hello-World-JavaScript'

title: String.trim()
summary: Removes whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) from both ends of a string. Essential for cleaning up user input — people often accidentally add spaces before or after what they type. Does not change the original string.

title: String.includes()
summary: Checks whether a string contains a specified substring. Returns true if found, false if not. Case-sensitive. Very useful for search filtering — you can check if a product name includes the user's search term, for example.

parameters.name: searchString
parameters.description: The substring to search for.

parameters.name: position
parameters.description: Optional. The position in the string to start searching from.

title: String.replace()
summary: Returns a new string with the first match of a search value replaced by a replacement string. The original string is not changed. To replace all occurrences, use replaceAll() or use a regex with the global flag /pattern/g.

parameters.name: searchValue
parameters.description: A string or regular expression to find. Only the first match is replaced (use replaceAll() for all).

parameters.name: replacement
parameters.description: The string to put in place of the match.

title: localStorage.setItem()
summary: Saves a key-value pair to the browser's localStorage — data that persists even after the browser tab is closed. Values must be strings, so use JSON.stringify() to save objects or arrays. Data is tied to the origin (domain + port).

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: A string key to identify the stored value.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to store — must be a string. Use JSON.stringify() for objects.

title: localStorage.getItem()
summary: Retrieves a value from localStorage using its key. Returns the stored string, or null if the key doesn't exist. If you stored an object with JSON.stringify(), you'll need to parse it back with JSON.parse().

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The string key of the item to retrieve.

title: window.location
summary: An object containing information about the current page URL, and methods to navigate the browser. You can read properties like href (full URL), pathname (/page/name), and search (?query=string), or use assign() and replace() to redirect users to another page.

parameters.name: .href
parameters.description: The full URL of the current page. Set it to navigate to a new URL.

parameters.name: .pathname
parameters.description: The path part of the URL, e.g. '/about' or '/products/1'.

parameters.name: .search
parameters.description: The query string, e.g. '?id=5&page=2'.

parameters.name: .assign(url)
parameters.description: Navigates to a new URL, keeping the current page in browser history.

parameters.name: .replace(url)
parameters.description: Navigates to a new URL, replacing the current page in history (can't go back).

parameters.name: .reload()
parameters.description: Reloads the current page.
